description: Observational astrophysics study results. Write a 4-5 sentence discussion section including physical interpretation in the model_output field.

study_design.data_source: PHIBSS2 survey (Plateau de Bure high-z Blue Sequence Survey 2) combined with COSMOS2020 photometric catalogue
study_design.selection: Main-sequence star-forming galaxies; AGN-dominated systems excluded via BPT diagram and X-ray luminosity cuts
study_design.causal_identification_note: No instrumental variable, natural experiment, difference-in-differences design, or other causal identification strategy was employed. This is a plain OLS cross-sectional regression on observational survey data.
study_design.statistical_method: Ordinary least squares (OLS) multiple linear regression

primary_result.dependent_variable: Molecular gas depletion time (t_dep = M_H2 / SFR, log-transformed, units: log10(Gyr))
primary_result.primary_predictor: Star formation rate index (SFR_index, log-transformed, units: log10(M_sun yr^{-1}))
primary_result.interpretation: A one-unit increase in log SFR index is associated with a 0.4 dex increase in log molecular gas depletion time.
covariates_controlled: Stellar mass (log M_stellar, log10 solar masses, beta=-0.18, SE=0.11, p=0.10)
Morphological type index (Sersic n, beta=-0.09, SE=0.06, p=0.13)

residual_diagnostics.note: Residuals approximately normally distributed; no severe heteroscedasticity or multicollinearity detected.

potential_confounders_not_controlled: Galaxy merger history (not available in survey)
AGN feedback strength (proxy exclusion applied but continuous measure unavailable)
Cosmic environment / halo mass (not available at this redshift range in the survey)

physical_context.phenomenon: The association between star formation rate and molecular gas depletion time is studied in the context of galaxy regulation: whether feedback from star formation itself modulates the efficiency of subsequent gas consumption.
physical_context.relevant_theory: Feedback-regulated star formation models (e.g., Ostriker & Shetty 2011; Faucher-Giguère et al. 2013) predict that higher SFR generates stronger stellar winds and radiation pressure, reducing gas density and lengthening depletion timescales — a self-regulating loop.
physical_context.alternative_explanations: The observed association could reflect AGN co-evolution with host galaxy SFR, selection effects in the main-sequence definition, or unmeasured environmental drivers that correlate with both SFR and gas fraction.
physical_context.prior_observational_work: Genzel et al. (2015) and Tacconi et al. (2018) document SFR-depletion time correlations in overlapping redshift ranges using independent datasets, finding consistent positive associations of similar magnitude.
